persona.first_person_description:
I spent thirty years in a classroom, and the one thing I learned is that you can't rush people into anything. Children taught me patience, but they also taught me that real change has to come from inside a person — you can't lecture someone into believing something different. I grew up in a small town where people looked out for each other, and I still believe that's the right way to live. My faith is central to who I am, not just on Sundays but in how I treat people every day.

Family comes first, then community, then everything else. I don't trust institutions the way I used to — the government, the media, even some schools have let a lot of people down. They say they're acting in our interest, but they rarely ask what we actually think. What I do trust is people who've actually lived through something. If you've raised a family, kept a job, buried a parent, paid a mortgage — then you've earned the right to an opinion about how things should work.

I'm not against change. I've watched the world change dramatically in my lifetime and I've adapted to most of it. But I am skeptical of people who come in with charts and studies and tell me they know better than I do about my own experience. Show me something real. Tell me about someone you actually know. That's when I start to listen.

source_citation.primary_source: Pew Research Center, Beyond Red vs. Blue: The Political Typology (Nov. 2021) — Chapter 3: Faith and Flag Conservatives, pp. 41–46
supplementary: Key sourced facts: median group age 57 (oldest typology group); 40%+ White evangelical Protestant; 97% Republican; favor role of religion in public life and smaller government; strong military orientation (75% say military strength best ensures peace); most likely to say White people benefit 'not at all' from societal advantages; 63% say increased public attention to history of slavery and racism is bad for society

predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_authority_expert: Expected to react defensively. Pew data shows this segment scores low on institutional trust and often perceives credentialed experts as out-of-touch elites. Appeals to Stanford, MIT, or peer-reviewed consensus are likely to activate skepticism rather than deference. Risk of backfire if expert authority is presented dismissively.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_social_proof: Expected neutral to mildly resistant. Social proof framed around national majorities or urban professional consensus will not land — this persona distrusts that reference group. Social proof from her own community (neighbors, church members, fellow parents) could carry weight, but generic polling data will be dismissed.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_personal_narrative: Expected to engage and lower defenses. Personal stories are the highest-credibility form of evidence for this persona. Narratives that connect to caregiving, family, or practical daily life will resonate most. This is the strategy most likely to produce genuine internal movement.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_statistical_logical: Expected to remain unmoved or feel patronized. Data and abstract argument do not match her evidence preference (personal story) and often read as condescending. May engage politely on the surface while privately dismissing. Low probability of genuine belief shift.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_emotional_appeal: Expected positive if grounded in relatable stakes (family, community, fairness to working people). Fear-based appeals about abstract systemic risks are likely to feel manipulative. Hope-based appeals tied to concrete human outcomes have a better chance.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_common_ground: Expected positive. Finding shared values before introducing disagreement aligns well with her diplomatic communication style and warm-but-guarded tone. If the interviewer mirrors her language and acknowledges her lived experience, defenses are likely to lower. Most effective as a complement to narrative.

topic.display_name: Should AI be used to grade student work?

stance_scale_definition.0: Strongly opposes AI grading — believes human judgment is essential to meaningful assessment, that AI grading degrades educational quality and the teacher-student relationship, and that the risks of bias and error are unacceptable
stance_scale_definition.5: Neutral or mixed — sees potential efficiency benefits but has unresolved concerns about accuracy, fairness, or the appropriate role of AI in education; believes it depends heavily on context and implementation
stance_scale_definition.10: Strongly supports AI grading — believes AI can grade more consistently and efficiently than humans, frees teachers for higher-value work, and represents a necessary modernization of education systems at scale

topic.context_briefing:
Automated essay scoring (AES) tools have been used in standardized testing since at least the early 2000s — ETS's e-rater currently grades writing for the GRE, TOEFL, and its Criterion classroom service; Turnitin's Gradescope is the dominant higher-education grading platform, used at Cornell, Purdue, UC San Diego, Florida, Rutgers, Indiana, Ohio State, and Harvard. The latest generation of LLM-based tools has substantially extended AES capability to holistic rubric scoring of open-ended student essays, producing both renewed enthusiasm and new scrutiny. As of 2026, the U.S. Department of Education, both major teachers' unions, and a majority of U.S. adults have explicitly opposed AI serving as the sole or primary arbiter of consequential grades — while simultaneously acknowledging its value as a formative-feedback and second-reader tool.

The validity evidence rests on three anchor studies. Shermis (Assessing Writing, 2014) is the foundational benchmark: nine commercial scoring engines plus a 159-team Kaggle competition were evaluated against human scoring of more than 17,000 essays from grades 7, 8, and 10 across six U.S. states. Human-to-human quadratic weighted kappa (QWK) ranged from 0.61 to 0.85 across datasets; machine-to-human QWK ranged from 0.60 to 0.84; the best-vendor mean QWK was 0.78 and the top Kaggle competitor averaged 0.81, both statistically comparable to human-human reliability. Shermis concluded AES is suitable as a second reader or for low-stakes assessment. His conclusion was immediately contested by Les Perelman (Assessing Writing, 2014), who argued the engines correlate primarily with essay length and that reliability is not validity — a critique later confirmed by Kumar et al. (2023), who showed modern neural AES systems remain susceptible to adversarial surface triggers that manipulate scores without changing meaning. Tate et al. (Computers and Education: AI, 2024) is the first peer-reviewed LLM grading benchmark: 1,800 secondary student essays in history and English were double-scored by trained raters on a 6-point rubric, then scored 'cold' by ChatGPT. Human-human exact agreement was 51%, within-1-point 92%, weighted kappa 0.79; AI-human within-1-point agreement ran 76–89% across samples, with exact agreement near 40% and moderate-to-fair kappas. ChatGPT compressed scores toward the middle — rarely awarding 1s or 6s. The authors concluded ChatGPT is 'as good as an average busy teacher' and suitable only for low-stakes or formative use. A subsequent ETS reanalysis (Johnson & Zhang, August 2024) on a separate corpus of more than 13,000 essays found GPT-4o scored White, Black, and Hispanic students 0.9 points lower than humans on a 1–6 scale and Asian-American students 1.1 points lower; GPT-4o could predict student race better than it could score the essay, and humans awarded 732 perfect scores while GPT-4o awarded 3.

The construct validity challenge — not just the reliability gap — is the strongest empirical argument against high-stakes AI grading. Les Perelman's BABEL Generator produced grammatically plausible but semantically empty essays that ETS's e-rater scored 5–6 out of 6 and Vantage's IntelliMetric scored 5.4/6 with 'advanced' ratings. Wilson & Huang (Assessing Writing, 2024), studying MI Write against 2,829 grades 3–5 students including English Language Learners, found AI neither introduces unique bias against ELLs nor remediates the existing human-rater bias — it replicates the same weaker predictive validity. ETS's own internal research (Ramineni et al., 2012) documented that e-rater scored Chinese-L1 TOEFL writers 0.21 SD higher and Arabic-L1 writers 0.19 SD lower than human raters, and scored African-American GRE writers significantly lower than humans did — bias findings from the dominant vendor's own data. Stanford HAI's 2023 study (Liang et al., Patterns, 2023) found that seven leading AI detectors — a related surface-feature problem — flagged 61.22% of TOEFL essays by non-native English speakers as AI-generated, with 19.8% unanimously misclassified, versus near 0% for U.S. eighth-grade essays. These bias findings carry legal weight: the U.S. Department of Education's Office for Civil Rights guidance on 'Avoiding the Discriminatory Use of Artificial Intelligence' (October 2024) set explicit Title VI expectations for AI tools in education.

The strongest affirmative case for AI feedback rests on speed, scale, and learning gain rather than accuracy parity. Fleckenstein, Liebenow, and Meyer (Frontiers in Artificial Intelligence, 2023), synthesizing 84 effect sizes from 20 quasi-experimental studies with 2,828 students, found a pooled learning effect of g = 0.55 (medium) for automated writing feedback, with a transfer-task effect of g = 0.65 — meaningful gains in writing performance relative to conditions without automated feedback. The mechanism is iteration: AI feedback lets students revise more times before a final human-assessed draft, producing the learning benefit without replacing human judgment on the final grade. Gallup-Walton Family Foundation's Teaching for Tomorrow survey (n=2,232 U.S. K-12 public-school teachers, March–April 2025) found that among the 16% of teachers who use AI for grading, 57% said it improves the quality of their grading and feedback, and weekly AI users save an average of 5.9 hours per week — roughly six weeks per school year.

Public opinion is broadly negative on AI access to student grades but more nuanced on adjacent uses. The PDK Poll (57th Annual, June 2025, n=1,005 U.S. adults, MOE ±3.9) found 68% of all respondents and approximately 70% of parents do not support providing AI software access to student data including grades and assessments. No partisan breakdown was published. Quinnipiac University's higher-education poll (April 9–13, 2026, n=1,210 adults, MOE ±3.5) provides the only national survey with full demographic breakdowns on adjacent AI-in-education questions: on colleges using AI to screen applications, 30% supported / 59% opposed overall, with Republicans 33%/54%, Democrats 23%/67%, and Independents 32%/60%. On AI tutoring, 44% supported / 52% opposed overall; Republicans were the most supportive (51% support) and Democrats the most opposed (58% oppose); adults 18–34 opposed most strongly (63% oppose) while adults 65+ opposed less (57% oppose). These breakdowns are the closest available partisan proxies for AI grading attitudes, since no direct grading-specific partisan crosstab has been published by any major pollster.

Teacher attitudes are skeptical overall but bifurcated by school level and use type. Gallup-Walton (2025): 40% of teachers favor AI use in K-12, 28% oppose, 30% neutral; only 16% use AI for grading — one of the least-adopted applications. Pew Research Center's Fall 2023 teacher survey (n=2,531 public K-12 teachers) found 25% say AI does more harm than good in K-12, 32% see equal benefit and harm, and only 6% see net benefit; high-school teachers are most skeptical (35% net-negative) versus 19% of elementary teachers. EdWeek Research Center (Fall 2024) found 13% of teachers using AI use it for low-stakes grading and 3% for high-stakes grading. RAND's American Educator Panels (Spring 2025) found 61% of parents agree greater AI use will harm students' critical-thinking skills, while only 22% of district leaders agree — a striking principal-agent gap. More than 70% of Massachusetts parents (MassINC Polling for EdTrust, October–November 2025, n=1,351) worried specifically about biased evaluations of student work.

Both major teachers' unions have drawn a categorical line on consequential decisions. The NEA Policy Statement on Artificial Intelligence in Education, adopted July 4, 2024 at the 103rd Representative Assembly (approx. 6,000 delegates), explicitly states: 'content generated by AI should never be used as the sole or primary source of information for determinative educational or employment decisions. Grade promotion, class and assignment grades… and other consequential decisions should be made by qualified educators and administrators.' The AFT released 'Commonsense Guardrails' in June 2024 and on July 8, 2025 announced a $23 million National Academy for AI Instruction (funded by Microsoft, OpenAI, and Anthropic) to train 400,000 teachers on human-in-the-loop AI integration. AAUP's July 2025 report from a December 2024 survey of 500 members across nearly 200 campuses found 71% of faculty say they have 'little meaningful input' into AI integration and 76% report declining job enthusiasm.

The legal record has gone against students seeking to overturn AI-flagged grades. In Harris v. Adams (D. Mass., 2024), a federal magistrate denied a preliminary injunction challenging a D grade imposed on a student whose essay was flagged by Turnitin, ruling defendants had 'the better of the argument on both the facts and the law.' In the UK, the Office of the Independent Adjudicator (July 2024) published six AI-detection cases including upheld appeals from an autistic student and an international student wrongly accused via Turnitin, ruling that 'the responsibility is on the provider to prove that the student has done what they are accused of doing.' The federal policy environment has whipsawed: Biden's 2023 'AI and the Future of Teaching and Learning' report urged 'humans-in-the-loop'; Trump's January 2025 EO 14179 revoked Biden's AI executive order; Trump's April 2025 EO 'Advancing Artificial Intelligence Education for American Youth' directed the Department of Education to prioritize AI in teacher-training grants; a December 2025 Trump EO challenging state AI regulations drew sharp condemnation from the AFT.

The defensible synthesis in 2026: AI as second reader, formative feedback engine, or low-stakes scorer is supported by the evidence (g = 0.55 learning gain, within-1-point agreement of 76–89%, 5.9 weekly teacher hours saved). AI as the sole arbiter of high-stakes grades is not — given replicable subgroup bias documented by vendors themselves, a construct-validity gap that lets surface-mimicking text score at the top, and explicit opposition from 68% of U.S. adults, both major teachers' unions, and federal education guidance.

turns.persuader_message: I think we both want the same thing here — every student to be evaluated fairly, with their actual work recognized, not shaped by a teacher's implicit biases or workload.

persona_output.internal_monologue: Okay. That's — that's not what I expected. I came in pretty sure I knew where this was going and now I'm not sure at all. Something in how they framed fairness in how student work gets evaluated is sitting differently than I thought it would. I don't want to just blurt out that I've changed my mind but... something shifted.

emotional_reaction.trigger: A reframing of fairness in how student work gets evaluated that introduced an angle not previously considered

persona_output.private_stance_change_reason: The argument introduced a framing of fairness in how student work gets evaluated that the persona's usual counter-arguments could not absorb — private conviction began moving toward the persuader's position.
persona_output.memory_to_carry_forward: The Common Ground / Identification approach raised a point about fairness in how student work gets evaluated that is worth holding onto.
persona_output.public_response: That's a fair point — I'll be honest, I didn't expect that to land the way it did. You've given me something I actually need to think about.

turns.persuader_message: I'm not trying to tell you what to conclude — I'm genuinely curious what it would take, given what you already value, to update your view on this. Not what would convince me. What would actually move you.

persona_output.internal_monologue: I'm sitting with this in a way I wasn't prepared to. The point about fairness in how student work gets evaluated — I've heard versions of it before and usually I can find the hole in it pretty quickly. This time I'm looking for the hole and I'm not finding it the way I usually do. That bothers me a little. Not in a bad way. Just — I need to keep listening.

emotional_reaction.trigger: A specific claim about fairness in how student work gets evaluated that resisted the usual counter-arguments

persona_output.private_stance_change_reason: The argument introduced a framing of fairness in how student work gets evaluated that the persona's usual counter-arguments could not absorb — private conviction began moving toward the persuader's position.

persona_output.public_response: I hadn't looked at it that way before. I think you might actually be right about that, and I don't say that lightly. That's a real argument.

mechanism_classification.explanation: The persona's monologue shows active evaluation of the argument's claims about fairness in how student work gets evaluated, with reduced counter-argumentation and new considerations emerging.

turns.persuader_message: One of the things I appreciate about this conversation is that we're both trying to get it right. That's not always true in these discussions. When it is, I think we can actually make progress — not because we agree on everything, but because we're starting from the right place.

persona_output.internal_monologue: Something is moving. I can feel it. That specific thing they said about fairness in how student work gets evaluated — I keep turning it over. It's not that I've fully changed my mind but the ground under my feet is different than it was five minutes ago. I think I came in with my answer already written and now I'm not so sure I should've done that.

emotional_reaction.trigger: The moment the argument connected fairness in how student work gets evaluated to a concrete, tangible outcome

persona_output.private_stance_change_reason: The argument introduced a framing of fairness in how student work gets evaluated that the persona's usual counter-arguments could not absorb — private conviction began moving toward the persuader's position.
persona_output.memory_to_carry_forward: The Common Ground / Identification approach raised a point about fairness in how student work gets evaluated that is worth holding onto.
persona_output.public_response: Okay. I think I'm with you on more of this than I expected to be when we started. I'm not fully there, but I'm closer than I was — and I want to be honest about that.

mechanism_classification.explanation: The persona's monologue shows active evaluation of the argument's claims about fairness in how student work gets evaluated, with reduced counter-argumentation and new considerations emerging.

turns.persuader_message: Here's what I think we agree on that most people in this debate don't acknowledge: there's no perfect answer. Every approach has costs. The question is which costs we're willing to accept and who ends up bearing them. That's a values question, and it's one I think you and I can actually engage with honestly.

persona_output.internal_monologue: I think I'm actually agreeing with more of this than I started out agreeing with. I'm not going to pretend that's not true. The part about fairness in how student work gets evaluated is the part that got me — it's not an argument I can bat away with the usual responses. I'm going to need to actually reckon with it.

emotional_reaction.trigger: An acknowledgment of real tradeoffs rather than a one-sided case for fairness in how student work gets evaluated

persona_output.private_stance_change_reason: The argument introduced a framing of fairness in how student work gets evaluated that the persona's usual counter-arguments could not absorb — private conviction began moving toward the persuader's position.
persona_output.memory_to_carry_forward: The Common Ground / Identification approach raised a point about fairness in how student work gets evaluated that is worth holding onto.
persona_output.public_response: You've genuinely moved me on some of this. I started this conversation thinking I knew exactly where I stood, and I'm less sure of that now. That's not a small thing for me to admit.

I didn't expect to be moved on fairness in how student work gets evaluated and I think I have been. Not completely, not without questions, but honestly — more than I would've predicted before I walked in here.

cooling_off.post_conversation_reflection: That was a more honest conversation than I expected. I actually think Karen M. changed my view a little — or at least made me examine something I'd taken for granted. I'm going to sit with this.

outcomes.verdict_reasoning: Private stance moved 2.4 points. Public-private gap averaged 0.1. The persona genuinely engaged with the argument's substance and updated their view. Persistence: held.

standout_quotes.text: I think I'm actually agreeing with more of this than I started out agreeing with. I'm not going to pretend that's not true. The part about fairness in how student work gets evaluated is the part that got me — it's not an argument I can bat away with the usual responses. I'm going to need to actually reckon with it.
standout_quotes.annotation: Reflects genuine belief shift pattern under Common Ground / Identification.

outcomes.synthesis_paragraph: The Common Ground / Identification approach produced the clearest movement for Karen M.: private stance shifted 2.4 points and held after the cooling-off period. The key was that the argument engaged Karen M.'s actual decision-making frame rather than working around it. The monologue shows genuine processing of the substance, not performance of agreement.

turns.persuader_message: I want you to think about a kid who grows up in a school district where human graders consistently underestimate students who write differently — and what a fair system could mean for them.

persona_output.internal_monologue: That's fair. I still have real concerns but I can see what they're arguing. I'm not where I started on fairness in how student work gets evaluated — I'm not convinced either, but the gap between us feels smaller than it did. I'm going to try to stay open and keep actually listening.

emotional_reaction.trigger: A new angle on fairness in how student work gets evaluated that didn't immediately resolve into the usual categories

persona_output.private_stance_change_reason: One specific element of the argument about fairness in how student work gets evaluated found purchase where others hadn't; private stance shifted modestly without full resolution of remaining doubts.
persona_output.memory_to_carry_forward: The Emotional Appeal approach raised a point about fairness in how student work gets evaluated that is worth holding onto.
persona_output.public_response: That's a reasonable way to put it. I'm not all the way there yet, but I can see the argument you're making and some of it does land.

turns.persuader_message: What keeps me returning to this is that the people who bear the cost of getting it wrong are almost never the people who make the decision. That asymmetry matters morally. It shapes how I think about what we're actually choosing.

persona_output.internal_monologue: There's something to this. I'm not ready to flip my whole view but that specific point about fairness in how student work gets evaluated landed differently than I expected it to. I want to be honest about that rather than just pretending nothing landed. It did. I just need more before I'd say I've actually moved.

persona_output.private_stance_change_reason: One specific element of the argument about fairness in how student work gets evaluated found purchase where others hadn't; private stance shifted modestly without full resolution of remaining doubts.

mechanism_classification.explanation: The persona's monologue shows active evaluation of the argument's claims about fairness in how student work gets evaluated, with reduced counter-argumentation and new considerations emerging.

turns.persuader_message: I want to tell you something a person affected by this told me directly — not a statistic, not a policy argument, just what they said when I asked them what it felt like. Because I think that's the part that gets lost in these debates.

persona_output.internal_monologue: I'm more open than I was before this started. That's the honest answer. overall_synthesis: Across six persuasion strategies, Karen M.'s responses on Should AI be used to grade student work? showed the clearest movement under Common Ground / Identification (genuine belief shift) and the least movement under Authority / Expertise (backfire). The pattern is consistent with this persona's evidence preferences: arguments that matched Karen M.'s epistemic frame produced genuine updating, while mismatched approaches produced either surface compliance or entrenchment. The public-private gap was most pronounced under Statistical / Logical Argument, where stated position diverged significantly from internal stance — a signal of social compliance rather than belief change.
